elements.sent-2411-001: Malvezzi \institute{Department of Information Engineering and Mathematics, University of Siena, Italy.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Nowadays, there is an increasing concern about the unsustainability of the take-make-dispose paradigm upon which traditional production and consumption systems are built.
elements.sent-2411-003: The concept of circular economy is gaining attention as a potential solution, but it is an emerging field still lacking analytical and methodological dynamics approaches.
elements.sent-2411-004: Hence, in this paper, firstly we propose a quantitative definition of circularity, namely, $\lambda$, predicated on compartmental dynamical thermodynamics, and then, we use it to state the optimization of the circularity $\lambda$ as an arg-max problem.
elements.sent-2411-005: By leveraging the derivation of Lagrange's equations of motion from the first law of thermodynamics, we apply the analytical mechanics approaches to circularity.
elements.sent-2411-006: Three examples illustrate the calculation of $\lambda$ for different settings of two compartmental networks.
elements.sent-2411-007: In particular, hypothesizing a repair stage followed by product reuse we highlight the memory property of $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Finally, robotic repair is proposed within this framework to pave the way for circular robotics as a new area of research in which the performance of a robotic system is measured against $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Raw materials and minerals are the constituents of any product, from the tiny transistor to the large football stadium.
elements.sent-2411-010: With the traditional (i.e., linear) economy, there are open issues regarding the criticality of several raw materials \cite{CRM-EU and regarding the management of end-of-life products \cite{EU-waste .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: The concept of circularity at the core of a circular economy (CE) is gaining importance to address both material supply uncertainties and waste generation \cite{EllenMacArthurFound .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The practical strategies to increase circularity at local and global scales are the so-called ``Rs'' such as reduce, reuse, repair, refurbish, and re-manufacture \cite{potting2017circular .}
elements.sent-2411-013: As a research field mainly rooted in business and environmental programs, CE currently lacks model-based, dynamics foundations.
elements.sent-2411-014: Hence, this paper contributes to exploiting system dynamics in circularity to provide mathematical methods and quantitative evaluations that can strengthen and clarify the knowledge of CE.
elements.sent-2411-015: Related Work: This brief paper builds on the systemic framework recently proposed by the authors \cite{zocco2024unification .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The framework is complementary to the state-of-the-art methodology of material flow analysis (MFA) \cite{cullen2022material,wang2024bayesian because leveraging ordinary differential equations, which underpin dynamical systems and control theories, rather than data analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In addition, this paper provides a measure of circularity similar to previous works \cite{basile2024measuring , but to the best of our knowledge, it is the first to do so through the lenses of analytical mechanics.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Consider the modeling approach of the Rankine cycle, which consists of applying the mass balance and the thermodynamic laws to each component of the cycle one at a time \cite{moran2010fundamentals .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Each component of the cycle is contained inside a control surface.
elements.sent-2411-020: Hence, each component of the cycle is a thermodynamic compartment contained inside a control surface.
elements.sent-2411-021: Given the generality of the mass balance and the laws of thermodynamics \cite{haddad2017thermodynamics,haddad2019dynamical , the modeling approach of the Rankine cycle can be generalized as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Let $c^k_{i,j}$ be the $k$-th thermodynamic compartment through which the material moves from compartment $i$ to compartment $j$.
elements.sent-2411-023: Thus, the Rankine cycle can be depicted as in Fig. \ref{fig:RankineCycle , where the control surfaces are indicated with the dashed lines on the left-hand side and with the rectangles on the right-hand side, and where $\beta$ is the working fluid.}
elements.sent-2411-024: The position of an infinitesimal mass element $\beta$ is tracked by a geometrical vector with respect to the origin $O$ of a reference frame.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.75\textwidth]{Figures/RankineCycle} \caption{Classic (left) and generalized (right) representations of the Rankine cycle. Figure adapted from \cite{zocco2024unification}.} \label{fig:RankineCycle}
elements.sent-2411-025: Note now that the compartmental representation on the right-hand side of Fig. \ref{fig:RankineCycle is a network of compartments.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Hence, it can be compactly represented by a graph, detailed in the following.
elements.sent-2411-027: A digraph $D$ in which each node or arc is associated with a weight is a weighted digraph.
elements.sent-2411-028: Using the compartmental perspective and the notion of digraph, we now introduce the definition of thermodynamical material network (TMN).
elements.sent-2411-029: Each compartment is indicated by a control surface and it is modeled using dynamical systems derived from a mass balance and/or at least one of the laws of thermodynamics \cite{haddad2019dynamical .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Specifically, $\mathcal{N = \mathcal{R \cup \mathcal{T}$, where $\mathcal{R \subseteq \mathcal{N}$ is the subset of compartments $c^k_{i,j}$ that store, transform, or use the target material, while $\mathcal{T \subset \mathcal{N}$ is the subset of compartments $c^k_{i,j}$ that move the target material between the compartments belonging to $\mathcal{R \subseteq \mathcal{N}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: A net $\mathcal{N}$ is associated with its weighted mass-flow digraph $M(\mathcal{N )$, which is a weighted digraph whose nodes are the compartments $c^k_{i,j \in \mathcal{R}$ and whose arcs are the compartments $c^k_{i,j \in \mathcal{T}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: For node-compartments $c^k_{i,j \in \mathcal{R}$ it holds that $i = j = k$, whereas for arc-compartments $c^k_{i,j \in \mathcal{T}$ it holds that $i \neq j$ because an arc moves the material from the node-compartment $c^i_{i,i}$ to the node-compartment $c^j_{j,j}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The orientation of an arc is given by the direction of the material flow.
elements.sent-2411-034: The superscript $k$ is the identifier of each compartment.
elements.sent-2411-035: The weight assigned to a node-compartment is the mass stock $m_k$ within the corresponding compartment, whereas the weight assigned to an arc-compartment is the mass flow rate $\dot{m _{i,j}$ from the node-compartment $c^i_{i,i}$ to the node-compartment $c^j_{j,j}$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The superscripts $k_v$ and $k_a$ in (\ref{def:TMNset ) are the $k$-th node and the $k$-th arc, respectively, while $n_c$ and $n_v$ are the total number of compartments and nodes, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Since $n_a$ is the total number of arcs, it holds that $n_c = n_v + n_a$ \cite{zocco2023thermodynamical,zocco2022circularity .}
elements.sent-2411-038: So far, we first recalled the well-known Rankine cycle, and then, we generalized its modeling approach as a sequence of thermodynamic compartments $\mathcal{N}$ (\ref{def:TMNset ) connected through the flow of a material $\beta$ of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Subsequently, we introduced the mass-flow digraph of $\mathcal{N}$, which provides a compact representation of $\mathcal{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-040: Now, since our goal is to design circular networks $\mathcal{N}$, we need to define a measure of circularity.
elements.sent-2411-041: We will do so after the following definition.
elements.sent-2411-042: The locations mentioned in Definition \ref{def:unsMassFlow , i.e., reservoirs, landfills, incinerators, and the environment, are thermodynamic compartments $c^k_{i,j \in \mathcal{N}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: We can now define the circularity of $\mathcal{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-044: In (\ref{eq:circularity ), $m_{u,b}$ is the total unsustainable mass transported in batches (e.g., solids transported on trucks), $\dot{m _{u,c}$ is the total unsustainable flow transported continuously (e.g., fluids transported through pipes), and $\Delta > 0$ is a constant interval of time introduced as a multiplying factor in order to convert the flow $\dot{m _{u,c}$ into a mass, and hence, to make the sum with $m_{u,b}$ physically consistent.}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The choice of $\Delta$ is arbitrary, but its value must be kept the same for any calculation of $\lambda(\mathcal{N )$ in order to make comparisons.}
elements.sent-2411-046: For simplicity and without loss of generality, we assume $\Delta = 1$ s.
elements.sent-2411-047: Thus, the design of circular systems $\mathcal{N}$ can be stated as \begin{equation}\mathcal{N}^* = \text{arg }\text{max} \quad \lambda(\mathcal{N}),\end{equation} which consists of finding the optimal compartmental network $\mathcal{N}$ that maximizes the circularity (\ref{eq:circularity ) or, equivalently, that minimizes the total unsustainable mass and flow.}
elements.sent-2411-048: In the next section, we will use analytical mechanics to model the dynamics of the material $\beta$ for a given $\mathcal{N}$.
elements.sent-2411-049: This will enable us to calculate the circularity $\lambda(\mathcal{N )$ (\ref{eq:circularity ), and then, to analyse how the circularity changes with different settings and configurations of $\mathcal{N}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Before doing so, the following result must be stated.
elements.sent-2411-051: Let $T$ be the kinetic energy, let $V$ be the potential energy, and let $L = T - V$ be the Lagrangian function of an element of material $\beta$ and mass $m$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Let $s$ be the coordinate associated with the element path and let $\xi$ be the sum of the nonconservative forces acting on the element.
elements.sent-2411-053: Thus, the Lagrange's equation of motion is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:LagrangeEquation} \frac{\text{d}}{\text{d}t}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{s}}\right) = \frac{\partial L}{\partial s} + \xi.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-054: The Lagrange's equation of motion (\ref{eq:LagrangeEquation ) can be derived from the first law of thermodynamics.}
elements.sent-2411-055: The proof is given in \cite{zocco2023thermodynamical , Equations (22)-(27).}
elements.sent-2411-056: From Proposition \ref{prop:LagrFromFirstLaw it follows that the application of Lagrange's equation (\ref{eq:LagrangeEquation ) to a mechanical system is an application of the first law of thermodynamics, and hence, a mechanical system can, in general, be represented as a thermodynamic compartment $c^k_{i,j \in \mathcal{N}$.}}}
elements.section-2411-003: Analytical Mechanics of Circularity through Examples
elements.sent-2411-058: Specifically, it begins with a linear configuration of $\mathcal{N}$ (Example 1), and subsequently, it hypothesizes material use reduction (Example 2) and robotic repair (Example 3) to analyse their effect on the circularity $\lambda(\mathcal{N )$ defined in Eq. (\ref{eq:circularity ).}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Consider the black nodes and the black arcs in Fig. \ref{fig:Example1 , which give the following linear system, namely, $\mathcal{N _{\text{l }$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{N}_{\text{l}} = \{c^1_{1,1}, c^2_{2,2}, c^3_{3,3}, c^4_{4,4},c^5_{1,2},c^6_{2,3},c^7_{3,4}\}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The representation of $\mathcal{N _{\text{l }$ in Fig. \ref{fig:Example1 is referred to as ``geometric digraph'', where the circles are the nodes, the arrows are the arcs, and the geometry of each arc is specified.}}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Specifically, $l_k$ is the length of the path travelled by the $k$-th compartment $c^k_{i,j}$.
elements.sent-2411-062: The configuration $\mathcal{N _{\text{l }$ is linear because: first, all the material is extracted from nonrenewable reservoirs; and second, all the material is sent to a landfill after its first use.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The dynamics of the cube of mass $m$ and material $\beta$ can be described by considering one compartment at a time starting from the nonrenewable reservoir ($c^1_{1,1}$).
elements.sent-2411-064: Assume that the cube leaves the reservoir in $t_0 = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Let $F_{\text{t }$ be the force of the truck ($c^5_{1,2}$), let $F_{\text{f }$ be the rolling friction and overall motion resistance, let $m_\text{t}$ be the mass of the truck, and let $m_{\text{c _5 = m_\text{t + m$ be the total mass of the truck compartment during the transportation of the cube.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Hence, the kinetic and the potential energies of the system are \begin{equation}T = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{c}_5} \dot{s}^2(t), \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}V = m_{\text{c}_5} g z(t) = m_{\text{c}_5} g (h - s(t) \sin \alpha), \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5,\end{equation} respectively.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}V = m_{c_5} g z(t) = m_{c_5} g (h - s(t) \sin \alpha), \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-067: Thus, with $\xi = F_{\text{t - F_{\text{f }$, the Lagrange's equation (\ref{eq:LagrangeEquation ) yields \begin{equation}\label{eq:c5-ddots} m_{\text{c}_5} \ddot{s}(t) = m_{\text{c}_5} g \sin \alpha + F_{\text{t}} - F_{\text{f}}, \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5,\end{equation} while the dynamics with respect to the reference frame is \begin{equation}\ddot{x}(t) = \ddot{s}(t) \cos \alpha, \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\ddot{y}(t) = 0, \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:c5-ddotz} \ddot{z}(t) = - \ddot{s}(t) \sin \alpha, \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq:c5-ddots} m_{c_5} \ddot{s}(t) = m_{c_5} g \sin \alpha + F_{t} - F_{f}, \quad 0 \leq s(t) \leq l_5,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-068: The positions and velocities of the material can be derived by integrating (\ref{eq:c5-ddots )-(\ref{eq:c5-ddotz ).}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Assume now that the truck arrives at the manufacturer ($c^2_{2,2}$) at $t_1$ and that a manufactured product made of the cube of material $\beta$ leaves the manufacturer at $t_2$ to be shipped to the retailer and the user ($c^3_{3,3}$), where $t_2 > t_1$.. This yields \begin{equation}\ddot{s}(t_1) = \dot{s}(t_1) = 0, s(t_1) = l_5, \quad t_1 < t < t_2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-070: Then, the dynamics of the shipping compartment $c^6_{2,3}$ is \begin{equation}m_{\text{c}_6} \ddot{s}(t) = F_{\text{s}} - F_{\text{d}}, \quad l_5 \leq s(t) \leq l_5 + l_6,\end{equation} where $m_\text{s}$ is the mass of the empty ship, $m_{\text{c _6 = m_\text{s + m$ is the mass of the loaded ship, and where $F_{\text{s }$ and $F_{\text{d }$ are the force of the ship and the drag, respectively.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation}m_{c_6} \ddot{s}(t) = F_{s} - F_{d}, \quad l_5 \leq s(t) \leq l_5 + l_6,\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-078: Finally, the garbage truck delivers the material to a landfill ($c^4_{4,4}$).
elements.sent-2411-079: Let us now quantify the circularity (\ref{eq:circularity ) of $\mathcal{N _{\text{l }$, namely, $\lambda_{\text{l }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Since the material is only transported in batches, we have that $\dot{m _{\text{u ,\text{c = 0$; moreover, there is a batch of mass $m$ exiting a nonrenewable reservoir and a batch of mass $m$ entering a landfill; thus, from Definition \ref{def:unsMassFlow it follows that $m_{\text{u ,\text{b = 2m$, and hence, \begin{equation}\lambda_{\text{l}} = - 2m.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Four key practices in a circular economy are reduce, reuse, repair, and recycle \cite{potting2017circular .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Let us see how reducing the material demand in Example \ref{sub:Example1 affects the circularity, which in this case is indicated with $\lambda_{\text{r _1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-083: A possible reduction could result from the choice of the manufacturer ($c^2_{2,2}$) to use 50\% of renewable material to make its products, e.g., to use 50\% bio-plastic and 50\% synthetic plastic.
elements.sent-2411-084: In this case, a mass of $m/2$ exits the nonrenewable reservoir, while a mass of $m$ enters the landfill (the whole product); thus, $m_{\text{u ,\text{b = \frac{m {2 + m$ and the circularity becomes \begin{equation}\lambda_{\text{r}_1} = - 1.5m.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Note that $\lambda_{\text{r _1 > \lambda_{\text{l }$, thus the reduction improves circularity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Another case of reduction occurs by reducing the amount of material required to make a product.
elements.sent-2411-087: In this second case of reduction, the manufacturer may need only 80\% of the material $\beta$ required in Example \ref{sub:Example1 ; this yields $m_{\text{u ,\text{b = 0.8m + 0.8m = 1.6m$, thus the circularity with reduction becomes \begin{equation}\lambda_{\text{r}_2} = - 1.6m.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-089: Consider the arcs and the nodes in black and in blue in Fig. \ref{fig:Example1 with the exception of $c^7_{3,4}$.}

elements.sent-2411-099: This phenomenon shows that circularity has a memory, that is, it must be defined with respect to a time horizon, which we call $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-100: To embed the memory into the notion of circularity (\ref{eq:circularity ), we use the notation $\lambda_{\phi,\text{l }$ and $\lambda_{\phi,\text{p }$ for the linear and the repair case, respectively; comparisons must be done for the same memory $\phi$ to be meaningful.}}}

elements.sent-2411-104: Extending the life cycle of materials is a key principle of CE \cite{EllenMacArthurFound and it is captured by $\lambda(\mathcal{N )$ with memory, namely, $\lambda_{\phi (\mathcal{N )$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: This paper proposes a physics-based, quantitative approach for assessing the circularity of a process or a system.
elements.sent-2411-106: In particular, a quantitative measure of circularity is proposed and its evaluation in three representative examples is shown.
elements.sent-2411-107: The proposed preliminary definition can be used to assess the circularity of a system and optimize its structure to minimize unsustainable mass transfers.
elements.sent-2411-108: Further studies on this topic aim to provide a non-dimensional, weighted definition of circularity and unsustainability, suitable for performing direct comparisons between different systems and plants, that, besides mass transfer, considers material typology in terms of availability and risks.
elements.sent-2411-109: Thanks to the physics-based process model, other parameters related to system dynamics can be evaluated and integrated into the circularity evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-110: Future investigations on this topic will focus on the integration of circularity assessment to take into account energetic efficiency, time constraints, and environmental impact.. \bibliographystyle{splncs04.}
